questions.context: A steam engine is a machine that converts the thermal energy of pressurized steam into mechanical motion. Early versions, built to pump water from deep mines, burned huge amounts of coal for little work. The chief fault lay in the single cylinder, which was heated by incoming steam and then cooled by water on every stroke. [■] Each cycle thus wasted most of its fuel reheating metal that the previous stroke had chilled. This heating and cooling was the obstacle the engineer James Watt set out to remove. In the 1760s he added a separate condenser, a chamber where steam could be cooled without cooling the main cylinder. [■] The cylinder could now stay hot from stroke to stroke, so far less coal was needed to raise fresh steam. [■] Watt later linked the piston to a rotating wheel, letting the engine turn machinery rather than merely pump. This rotary motion transformed the engine from a mine pump into the driving force of mills and, eventually, locomotives. [■] Workshops that had depended on flowing rivers could now be built wherever coal was available, freeing manufacturers from fixed water sources. However, the machines stayed heavy and burned fuel steadily, so they suited large enterprises far better than small workshops that could not justify the expense.

options.A: frequent
options.B: principal
options.C: hidden
options.D: technical

questions.context: Buzz pollination, also known as sonication, is a process in which certain bees vibrate their bodies to shake loose pollen that a flower keeps tightly enclosed. In many flowering plants, the pollen sits inside tube-shaped anthers that open only through a minute pore at the very tip. [■] Ordinary contact, the means by which most pollen passes between blossoms, cannot draw these hidden grains out. This concealment explains why such plants depend on a specialized behavior rather than on passive contact. A visiting bee anchors its jaws onto the anther and rapidly contracts its flight muscles without beating its wings, producing vibrations of a particular frequency. [■] The shaking travels through the anther and drives a cloud of pollen out through the narrow pore, dusting the insect's body. [■] This mechanism matters because several major crops, among them tomatoes, blueberries, and eggplants, hold their pollen in exactly this way. Honeybees, the most familiar of pollinators, cannot sonicate, so growers increasingly rely on bumblebees and other wild species to set fruit. [■] However, the wild bees capable of buzzing are declining across many regions, and their loss could quietly reduce harvests long before the cause became obvious.

questions.context: A river delta is a landform that develops where a river deposits its sediment upon entering a calmer body of water, such as a sea or a lake. Deltas take shape only where the river supplies particles faster than waves and currents can sweep them away. [■] Where that balance holds, the gathering sediment builds outward into the triangular fan for which the feature is named. This outward growth depends on a simple change in the moving water's energy. As the channel meets the standing water and slows, it can no longer transport its load, dropping the heaviest sand near the mouth and carrying the finest clay farthest offshore. [■] The main channel repeatedly splits into smaller branches, called distributaries, that spread the sediment across a broad front. [■] These fertile, level plains, some of the most crowded places on Earth, have long attracted dense human settlement and intensive farming. Yet the same deltas are unusually fragile: because they sit barely above sea level, they sink whenever the supply of fresh sediment is interrupted. [■] Dams built far upstream now trap sediment in their reservoirs, and many of these deltas are eroding and subsiding faster than the weakened rivers can rebuild them.

questions.context: Tonality is the system of organizing Western music around a single central pitch, known as the tonic, toward which the other notes seem to lean. Within a given key, this tonic anchors the entire passage, giving listeners a clear sense of a musical home. The remaining pitches of the scale are heard in relation to it, some sounding restful and others generating a pull that seeks release. This pull arises most strongly from the way chords are built and ordered. A chord constructed on the fifth note of the scale, the dominant, contains tones that clash mildly with one another and sound unresolved. Listeners feel this instability as a demand for motion, and the dominant characteristically gives way to the tonic, a shift that the ear accepts as a satisfying arrival. This expectation is something composers have long manipulated to shape emotion. By delaying the return to the tonic, a composer can stretch tension across many measures, making the eventual resolution feel hard-won. However, by the early twentieth century some composers deliberately abandoned any single tonic, arguing that centuries of tonal habit had begun to limit rather than enable fresh expression.

questions.context: Globalization is the process through which economies, cultures, and populations across the world become increasingly connected by the movement of goods, people, and ideas. In its earliest form, this connection ran along slow overland and maritime routes that carried silk, spices, and precious metals between distant regions. Such exchange touched only a thin layer of society, since transport was costly and confined largely to rare, high-value luxuries. These early connections deepened dramatically during the nineteenth century, when steamships, railways, and the telegraph slashed the time and cost of moving both cargo and information. Trade widened to bulk goods such as grain and coal, yet the new system was uneven, tied closely to colonial empires, and it broke down amid the world wars and the economic depression of the early twentieth century. This expanded system took firmer hold after the mid-twentieth century, when standardized shipping containers and, later, digital communication made worldwide exchange faster and far cheaper than ever before. Everyday manufactured goods now cross whole oceans routinely. However, this dense integration also transmits shocks quickly, so a disruption in one region can ripple through the entire global network within days.

Such exchange touched only a thin layer of society, since transport was costly and confined largely to rare, high-value luxuries. These early connections deepened dramatically during the nineteenth century, when steamships, railways, and the telegraph slashed the time and cost of moving both cargo and information. Trade widened to bulk goods such as grain and coal, yet the new system was uneven, tied closely to colonial empires, and it broke down amid the world wars and the economic depression of the early twentieth century. This expanded system took firmer hold after the mid-twentieth century, when standardized shipping containers and, later, digital communication made worldwide exchange faster and far cheaper than ever before. Everyday manufactured goods now cross whole oceans routinely. However, this dense integration also transmits shocks quickly, so a disruption in one region can ripple through the entire global network within days.
questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about shipping containers and digital communication?

options.A: They were developed largely to serve military supply needs.
options.B: They erased the remaining differences between national economies.
options.C: They made global trade far slower and more expensive than ever before.
options.D: They let goods and information circulate more cheaply than in earlier eras.

questions.subtopic: how chunking improves working memory capacity
questions.context: Working memory is the mental workspace where you hold and manipulate information for a few seconds, a phone number you're dialing, a sentence you are reading, the steps of a math problem. And it turns out this workspace is remarkably limited. In the nineteen-fifties, psychologist George Miller estimated that most people can juggle only about seven separate items at once, give or take two. So how do expert waiters memorize a dozen dinner orders, or chess masters recall entire boards at a glance? The answer is a process called chunking. Chunking means grouping individual pieces of information into larger, meaningful units. Consider the letters F, B, I, C, I, A, N, A, S, A. As ten separate letters, that string overwhelms your working memory. But regroup them into FBI, CIA, NASA, and suddenly you are holding just three chunks instead of ten. Each chunk still contains the same letters, yet it occupies only one slot. Researchers demonstrated this with a college student who trained for months to repeat back long strings of random digits. An average person manages about seven digits; this student eventually reached over eighty. His secret wasn't a bigger memory. He was a competitive runner, so he recoded the digits into race times, three or four numbers at a time. The key insight is that chunking does not expand the number of slots in working memory. It expands what each slot can hold. This is why expertise matters so much: the more you know about a subject, the larger and more efficient your chunks become. A novice sees scattered details, while an expert sees a few rich, organized patterns.
questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How grouping information lets people overcome working memory limits
options.B: The story of a student who memorized over eighty digits
options.C: Why long-term memories fade more slowly than short-term ones
options.D: How government agencies such as the FBI train new staff

questions.subtopic: how folk melodies were absorbed into nationalist compositions
questions.context: In the nineteenth century, a powerful idea swept through European music: that a composer's work should capture the spirit of a particular nation. This movement is known as musical nationalism, and one of its central techniques was drawing on folk melodies, the traditional songs of ordinary people in the countryside. Composers wanted their symphonies and operas to sound distinctly Norwegian, or Czech, or Hungarian, rather than simply echoing the international style centered in Vienna and Paris. Now, absorbing folk material was rarely as simple as copying a tune note for note. Take the Hungarian composer Béla Bartók. Early in the twentieth century, he traveled through remote villages with a phonograph, recording thousands of peasant songs before they vanished. But when he used them in his compositions, he transformed them, reharmonizing the melodies with modern, dissonant chords the original singers would never have imagined. The folk tune supplied the raw material; the composer supplied a new frame. Interestingly, some so-called folk themes weren't authentic at all. Certain composers simply invented melodies in a folk style, and audiences couldn't tell the difference, accepting them as genuine national heritage. This reveals something important: what mattered was not strict authenticity but the feeling of national identity the music evoked. By weaving these melodies, real or imagined, into concert works, composers helped forge a sense of shared cultural belonging at a time when many nations were struggling to define themselves. The folk song, once sung in a village square, became a symbol of the nation on the grand stage of the concert hall.
questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

questions.subtopic: how folk melodies were absorbed into nationalist compositions
questions.context: In the nineteenth century, a powerful idea swept through European music: that a composer's work should capture the spirit of a particular nation. This movement is known as musical nationalism, and one of its central techniques was drawing on folk melodies, the traditional songs of ordinary people in the countryside. Composers wanted their symphonies and operas to sound distinctly Norwegian, or Czech, or Hungarian, rather than simply echoing the international style centered in Vienna and Paris. Now, absorbing folk material was rarely as simple as copying a tune note for note. Take the Hungarian composer Béla Bartók. Early in the twentieth century, he traveled through remote villages with a phonograph, recording thousands of peasant songs before they vanished. But when he used them in his compositions, he transformed them, reharmonizing the melodies with modern, dissonant chords the original singers would never have imagined. The folk tune supplied the raw material; the composer supplied a new frame. Interestingly, some so-called folk themes weren't authentic at all. Certain composers simply invented melodies in a folk style, and audiences couldn't tell the difference, accepting them as genuine national heritage. This reveals something important: what mattered was not strict authenticity but the feeling of national identity the music evoked. By weaving these melodies, real or imagined, into concert works, composers helped forge a sense of shared cultural belonging at a time when many nations were struggling to define themselves. The folk song, once sung in a village square, became a symbol of the nation on the grand stage of the concert hall.

options.B: He taught the original melodies to students in Vienna
options.C: He reharmonized them with modern, dissonant chords
options.D: He simplified them to match traditional peasant singing

questions.subtopic: the anthropology of food taboos across societies
questions.context: Every human society draws a line between what counts as food and what does not. A substance that one culture treats as a delicacy, another may find utterly repulsive. These prohibitions are what anthropologists call food taboos, and they are far more revealing than they first appear. A food taboo is a cultural rule forbidding the eating of certain animals or plants, even when those items are nutritious and readily available. Consider the pig. In several Middle Eastern traditions, pork is strictly forbidden, while in parts of East Asia it's a dietary staple. Why the difference? Anthropologists have offered two competing explanations. The materialist view, associated with Marvin Harris, argues that taboos make practical sense: pigs compete with humans for grain and water and are poorly suited to hot, arid climates, so avoiding them was economically sound. The symbolic view, associated with Mary Douglas, takes a different angle. She argued that cultures sort animals into orderly categories, and creatures that don't fit neatly, that seem to cross boundaries, get labeled unclean. A pig, which has cloven hooves but does not chew its cud, is in this framework an anomaly. What both approaches share is the recognition that taboos are not arbitrary quirks. They encode a group's environment, its history, and its way of organizing the world. When you learn why a community refuses a particular food, you are really learning how it draws the boundaries of its own identity. That is why an anthropologist studying a single dietary rule can end up uncovering an entire worldview.
questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why pigs are specifically avoided in some Middle Eastern traditions
options.B: How different climates determine which crops a society grows
options.C: What food taboos reveal about a culture's worldview
options.D: How anthropologists sort animals into physical classification categories

questions.subtopic: how language shapes the way we perceive color
questions.context: Look at a rainbow, and you might assume everyone sees exactly the same bands of color. But the words a language gives us for color can actually influence how quickly we notice differences between shades. This idea connects to a broader theory in linguistics called linguistic relativity, the notion that the language we speak can shape aspects of how we perceive the world. Color is one of the clearest testing grounds. English treats blue as a single category, but Russian has no single word for it. Instead, Russian speakers must distinguish goluboy, a light blue, from siniy, a dark blue, the way English speakers separate pink from red. Researchers wondered whether this linguistic difference affects perception itself. In one well-known study, participants looked at squares of blue and had to quickly pick which of two shades matched a target. Russian speakers were measurably faster than English speakers at telling light blue from dark blue, because their language forces them to mark that boundary. When the two shades fell within a single Russian category, that advantage disappeared. Now, this doesn't mean English speakers can't see the difference. Of course they can. The effect is subtle, a matter of milliseconds. But it suggests that the categories built into our vocabulary act like a lens, nudging our attention toward certain distinctions. Language doesn't trap us in a fixed way of seeing, but it does gently tune what we notice first. And color is only the beginning. Researchers apply the very same questions to how different languages carve up space, time, and even emotion.
questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How Russian separates light blue from dark blue
options.B: Why rainbows contain a fixed number of color bands
options.C: How people match colored squares in laboratory experiments
options.D: How the words a language offers can influence color perception
